coredata.dc:title: Synergistic enhancement of potassium permanganate-induced chemiluminescence by nitrogen and silver carbon dots and formaldehyde for selective detection of tannin
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Zixuan
Xie, Jiaoyan
Han, Suqin
coredata.dc:description: In this study, nitrogen and silver doped carbon dots (NAg-CDs) were synthesized via a simple and environmentally friendly hydrothermal one-pot method and were utilized in combination with formaldehyde to enhance potassium permanganate-induced chemiluminescence (CL). Remarkably, a chemiluminescent enhancement of approximately 448-fold was observed in the NAg-CDs-KMnO4-HCHO system than in the KMnO4-HCHO system. The enhanced CL intensity of the NAg-CDs-KMnO4-HCHO system can be significantly quenched by tannin. On this basis, we developed a simple, rapid, sensitive, and selective flow injection CL method for the detection of tannin, featuring a linear range of 0.4–10.0 μM and a detection limit of 43.0 nM. Furthermore, we effectively applied this method to the detection of tannin in red wine and tea samples, and the standard recoveries were 98.6 %104.9 %.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen- and silver-doped carbon dots
Chemiluminescence
Permanganate
Formaldehyde
Tannin

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: UV–vis and FL properties of NAg-CDs
Enhancement of NAg-CDs on KMnO4-HCHO reaction
Optimization of experimental conditions for TA detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optical properties of NAg-CDs. (a) UV–vis absorption of NAg-CDs, (b) FL emission spectra of NAg-CDs at different excitation wavelengths.

(a) CL kinetic curves of (1) KMnO4-HCHO system, (2) NAg-CDs-KMnO4 system, (3) NAg-CDs-KMnO4-HCHO system and (4) NAg-CDs-KMnO4-HCHO-TA system. (b) CL spectra of (1) KMnO4-HCHO system, (2) NAg-CDs-KMnO4 system, (3) NAg-CDs-KMnO4-HCHO system and (4) NAg-CDs-KMnO4-HCHO-TA system. (c) CL intensity of the NAg-CDs-KMnO4-HCHO system (grey column) and the NAg-CDs-KMnO4-HCHO-TA system (pink column) after adding several free radical scavengers (1.0 mM).
(a) CL intensity-time curves of NAg-CDs-KMnO4-HCHO system at different concentrations of TA, (b) linear relationship between CL intensity and log of the TA concentrations from 0.4 to 10.0 μM.

head.ce:title: Synergistic enhancement of potassium permanganate-induced chemiluminescence by nitrogen and silver carbon dots and formaldehyde for selective detection of tannin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 030000, Shanxi, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 030000, Shanxi, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, nitrogen and silver doped carbon dots (NAg-CDs) were synthesized via a simple and environmentally friendly hydrothermal one-pot method and were utilized in combination with formaldehyde to enhance potassium permanganate-induced chemiluminescence (CL). Remarkably, a chemiluminescent enhancement of approximately 448-fold was observed in the NAg-CDs-KMnO4-HCHO system than in the KMnO4-HCHO system. The enhanced CL intensity of the NAg-CDs-KMnO4-HCHO system can be significantly quenched by tannin. On this basis, we developed a simple, rapid, sensitive, and selective flow injection CL method for the detection of tannin, featuring a linear range of 0.4–10.0 μM and a detection limit of 43.0 nM. Furthermore, we effectively applied this method to the detection of tannin in red wine and tea samples, and the standard recoveries were 98.6 %104.9 %.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: In this work, NAg-CDs were used in combination with HCHO to enhance KMnO4 CL. Remarkably, the NAg-CDs-KMnO4-HCHO system exhibited CL about 448 times stronger than the KMnO4-HCHO system. The CL intensity was significantly quenched by tannin. On this basis, we developed a simple, rapid, sensitive and selective FI-CL method for the detection of tannin in red wine and tea.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Chemiluminescence (CL) is the emission of light resulting from electronic state transitions during a chemical reaction. CL has garnered significant attention for its sensitive analyte detection due to its unique characteristics: independence from an external excitation light source, simple instrumentation, ease of operation, low background interference, high sensitivity, and rapid response. Various CL reagents such as luminol, lucigenin, peroxalate, SNAzyme, KMnO4, Ce(IV), H2O2 and carbon dots (CDs) have aroused great research interest (He et al., 2023; Sun et al., 2019). Among them, the acidic KMnO4-induced CL is of great importance in microanalysis (Adcock et al., 2014). The reaction of KMnO4 in an acidic solution leads to a visible red emission, and there is strong evidence that it originates from an electronically excited Mn(II) species (Adcock et al., 2008; Adcock et al., 2014; Hindson et al., 2010). Low molecular weight aldehydes, notably formaldehyde (HCHO), are used as enhancers for KMnO4 CL. Typically, the enhancer is added to the reactant solutions shortly before detection, as it directly reacts with KMnO4 (which is observed as a constant background emission). It has been reported that the reaction between acidic KMnO4 and aldehydes generates Mn(III), which can reduce to Mn(II)* without a stabilizing agent. The reaction forms a permanganate ester intermediate via KMnO4 nucleophilic addition to aldehydes, with its oxidative decomposition as the rate-determining step in the overall process (Jaky and Szammer, 1997).

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new class of nanomaterials consisting of discrete, quasi-spherical nanoparticles with sizes below 10 nm (Baker and Baker, 2010). CDs exhibit intriguing properties such as excitation wavelength-dependent photoluminescence behavior, good physicochemical, photochemical and thermal stability, and low toxicity. As versatile nanomaterials, CDs offer enormous potential for a wide range of applications, including chemical sensing, biosensing, bioimaging, photocatalysis, and electrocatalysis (Lim et al., 2015). Combining low cost and ease of synthesis, CDs provide substantial advantages for diverse applications. Furthermore, CDs hold significant potential for CL reactions. Various CL amplification systems utilizing CDs have been extensively explored and applied for the detection of substances such as metal ions, anions, small molecules, reactive oxygen, DNA, etc. (Shen et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2019). Moreover, the introduction of nonmetal and metal heteroatoms (N, S, P, Ag, Cu, Fe, etc) into CDs has significantly improved the unique properties of CDs. This includes the regulation of the internal electronic structure, the creation of numerous active sites, and improvement in selectivity and fluorescence quantum yield. These advancements endow CDs with high utility and versatile applications across various fields (Ansari et al., 2021; Du and Guo, 2016; Li et al., 2022). To date, various singly, doubly, and multiply doped CDs have been developed in different fields, including CL quantitative analysis (Ansari et al., 2021; Li et al., 2022). In CL systems, doped CDs are usually used as catalysts, emitters and energy receptors to improve their sensitivity, stability, and selectivity (Amjadi et al., 2018; Delnavaz and Amjadi, 2020; Shah et al., 2019; Shokri and Amjadi, 2022).

Tannin (TA) is a bitter and astringent natural phenolic substance widely distributed in various plants such as seeds, bark, wood, leaves and fruit peels. It is increasingly utilized in food processing, cosmetics, pharmaceuticals, and water treatment due to its antioxidant, radical-scavenging, anti-inflammatory, and antibacterial properties (Sharma et al., 2019). TA is the essence of red wine, forming its structural “skeleton”, stabilizing texture, and polymerizing and stabilizing pigment substances, ultimately imparting a perfect and vibrant color to the wine. It undergoes reactions with other compounds in the wine, leading to the creation of new substances that increase the complexity of the wine. TA in the tea also gives the drink an astringent taste. Teas with high TA content exhibit a bitter taste accompanied by a strong astringency, especially in green and black teas. Therefore, TA content in tea and red wine not only affects the taste but is an important criterion for evaluating the quality of tea and red wine. TA has an antinutritional effect, can hinder digestion and metabolism, and prevents the absorption of iron into the bloodstream, potentially leading to various health problems. TA has low toxicity, but excessive intake can cause vomiting, abdominal pain, diarrhea, and fatal circulatory disorders. Therefore, it is of great importance to develop a reliable and simple TA determination method for food analysis. Various methods are known for the quantitative measurement of TA content, including photoelectrochemical sensors (Silva et al., 2018), electrochemistry (Yılmaz et al., 2016), fluorescence (FL) (Chang et al., 2010; Liu et al., 2019), colorimetry (Dewi et al., 2014), CL (Corominas et al., 2002; Li et al., 2015), and diffuse reflection (Lima et al., 2012).

In this work, nitrogen and silver doped carbon dots (NAg-CDs) were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method, and the NAg-CDs showed strong CL emission in a solution with KMnO4, HCHO, and H2SO4. Upon introducing TA, the CL intensity significantly decreased, showcasing high selectivity and sensitivity. Hence, a simple, rapid, and sensitive flow injection (FI) CL method was developed for TA detection in red wine and tea samples. The NAg-CDs-KMnO4-HCHO CL system was investigated by CL spectroscopy, FL spectroscopy, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, and radical scaverger experiments. Additionally, a probable mechanism was proposed.

Fructose (C6H12O6), urea (CH4N2O), and tannic acid (TA) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). HCHO was purchased from Roche Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). KMnO4 and AgNO3 were purchased from Tianjin Fengchuan Chemical Reagent Company (Tianjin, China). Analytical reagents and ultra-pure water were used in the experiment. A stock solution of 1.0 mM TA was prepared by dissolving an appropriate amount of TA standard in ultrapure water. The calibration curve was prepared using TA standard solutions ranging from 0.1 to 20.0 μM by appropriate dilution of the stock solution with ultrapure water. TA solutions for standard addition were then prepared by diluting the stock solution with ultrapure water.

An IFIS-C flow injection sampler (Xi’an Remax Analytical Instrument Co. Ltd., Shannxi, China) and a BPCL ultraweak luminescence analyzer (Institute of Biophysics, Chinese Academy of Science, Beijing, China) were used to perform the CL assay. The photomultiplier tube (PMT) voltage was set to 600 V (unless specifically mentioned). The FL spectrum was recorded by a CaryEclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian, USA). The UV–vis absorption spectrum was recorded by a Cary 5000 spectrofluorometer (Varian, USA). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was recorded by a Varian 660-IR spectrometer (Varian, USA). The morphology of the NAg-CDs was studied by a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL. Co., ltd, Japan). The energy dispersive spectrum (EDS) was recorded by a JSM-7500 F scanning electron microscope (JEOL, Japan).

The synthesis method of NAg-CDs adopted a green and simple one-pot method, with minor adjustments based on the previous literature (Yao et al., 2020). Fructose (0.5 g) and urea (1.0 g) were dissolved in 13.5 mL water. Then, 1.5 mL of 0.01 M AgNO3 solution was added to the above solution. The solution was magnetically stirred for 15 min, transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, and held at 180 °C for 5 h. After natural cooling, centrifugation was performed at 10,000 rpm for 5 min, and the supernatant was collected and filtered again using a 0.22 µm filter membrane to obtain the purified NAg-CDs solution. The prepared NAg-CDs solution was stored in refrigerator for later use. Undoped CDs (fructose as raw material) were also prepared according to the procedure described above.

Tea and red wine were purchased from local supermarkets. They were pre-treated according to the literature (Chang et al., 2010; Liu et al., 2019). The red wine sample was used for determination after 50-fold dilution. A 3.0 g of tea leaves was brewed in 100 mL of water for 10 min, then filtered, cooled to room temperature, transferred to a 250 mL volumetric flask, and diluted with water to the mark. The extraction solution of the tea sample was diluted 100-fold for the experiment. The standard addition method was used to test the feasibility of this method.

The proposed CL method was used for TA detection, and the flow path of the FI-CL system was shown in Fig. S1. HCHO and NAg-CDs were delivered into the flow cell through a sampling valve by peristaltic pump 1. Water carrier, various concentrations of TA, and KMnO4 solution were transported into the flow cell by peristaltic pump 2 at a flow rate of 2.8 mL/min. After all solutions flowed into the flow cell, the CL reaction occurred, and the generated CL signal was recorded using a BPCL ultraweak luminescence analyzer. An end-window PMT supplied with −600 V was used for the CL measurement. TA concentration was quantitatively detected by the relative CL intensity ΔI = I 0 − I (I 0 and I were the CL signal value in the absence and presence of TA, respectively).

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis and FL properties of NAg-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of the NAg-CDs were investigated by UV–vis absorption spectrum and FL spectrum ( Fig. 1). The UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 1a) demonstrates obvious absorption peaks at 275 nm, which may be ascribed to the π-π * transition of the aromatic CC bond. A prominent broad absorption from 275 to 500 nm is likely attributed to the formation of excited states induced by the heteroatoms (Gong et al., 2015). The single peak means that they are present in the form of a complex after doping with N and Ag, rather than being present in separate substances (Yao et al., 2020). The NAg-CDs displayed intense and intrinsic FL in the aqueous solution. The FL spectra showed the typical excitation-dependent emission wavelength properties of NAg-CDs, as shown in Fig. 1b. The excitation wavelength-dependent FL demonstrated the influence of both nanodots size variation and a considerable distribution of emissive trap sites on each NAg-CD (Cao et al., 2007; Dong et al., 2013). The FL intensity sharply increased when the excitation wavelength increased from 350 to 400 nm, reaching a maximum of 470 nm when the excitation wavelength was 400 nm. Subsequently, the FL intensity gradually decreased when the excitation wavelength was changed from 400 to 430 nm. The quantum yield of the NAg-CDs was estimated to be 45.8 % using quinine sulfate as a reference.

The morphology and size of the prepared NAg-CDs were observed by TEM, as shown in Fig. S2a. The NAg-CDs had a spherical structure with a particle size of 5.0 ± 1.5 nm, and displayed good dispersion in an aqueous solution. The EDS spectrum (Fig. S2b) showed the elemental composition of NAg-CDs. The mass fraction and atomic percentage of C, O and Ag were 76.05, 22.3, 1.6 and 81.79, 18.1 and 0.2, respectively. A 3.0 keV peak in the energy spectrum, corresponding to Ag, indicates successful Ag doping in NAg-CDs (Yao et al., 2020). The FT-IR spectrum of NAg-CDs was measured to verify the functional groups (Fig. S2c). The peaks at 2927 and 2856 cm−1 were identified as the stretching vibrations of the C–H group. The absorption band at 3432 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibration of O–H and N–H bonds. The peak at 1637 cm−1 was ascribed to the CC bond. The absorption peak at 1400 cm−1 could be caused by the stretching vibration of C–N (Li et al., 2020; Yao et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2023). The result suggested the presence of numerous oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of NAg-CDs, which may provide binding sites for TA in further tests and contribute to the excellent water solubility of NAg-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhancement of NAg-CDs on KMnO4-HCHO reaction
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the enhancing effect of NAg-CDs on KMnO4-HCHO CL system, we measured the kinetic CL curves of KMnO4-HCHO, NAg-CDs-KMnO4, and NAg-CDs-KMnO4-HCHO systems, respectively ( Fig. 2a). It was obvious that KMnO4 with HCHO produced ultra-weak CL, while the addition of NAg-CDs led to a significantly intensified CL emission, which was about 440 times that of the KMnO4-HCHO system, indicating that NAg-CDs had excellent sensitization property for KMnO4-HCHO reaction. Control experiments were performed with fructose, urea, AgNO3 and fructose-urea-AgNO3 solutions at the concentration used for the preparation of NAg-CDs in the KMnO4-HCHO system (Fig. S3a). The results showed no enhancement effect for the above reagents and confirmed that the enhancement of CL intensity was due to the NAg-CDs. The CL enhancement by NAg-CDs to the KMnO4-HCHO system was compared to that of undoped CDs (Fig. S3b). The results revealed that the NAg-CDs had a much stronger enhancement effect on the CL signal than the undoped CDs.

To generate the strong CL signal in the designed FI system, three reagent mixing modes were tested (Fig. S3b). In the first mode, HCHO was injected into the mixture of KMnO4-NAg-CDs, in the second mode, NAg-CDs were injected into the mixture of KMnO4-HCHO solution, and in the last mode, KMnO4 was injected into the mixture of NAg-CDs-HCHO. The highest CL intensity was observed in the last mode.

Upon introducing TA to the NAg-CDs-KMnO4-HCHO system, the CL intensity decreased significantly (Fig. 2a), allowing TA determination with this CL system. Fig. S3c showed the UV–vis spectra of KMnO4, TA, and KMnO4-TA. The spectrum of KMnO4 has two characteristic absorption peaks at 525 and 545 nm, and the absorption peaks of the spectrum of the KMnO4-TA system disappeared with the addition of TA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of experimental conditions for TA detection
ce:section.ce:para: The reagent concentrations in this experiment were optimized for TA determination. The influence of KMnO4 concentration in the range of 0.04–0.4 mM was studied (Fig. S4a). At a 0.1 mM KMnO4 concentration, TA had the largest inhibition on the CL signal (ΔI). Therefore, 0.1 mM KMnO4 was selected as the optimal concentration for the following analysis. HCHO was one of the commonly used enhancers in the KMnO4 system, and its concentration played an important role. The variation of the CL emission with the HCHO concentration was studied in the 1–7 % range (Fig. S4b). The ΔI increased with an increasing concentration of HCHO up to a concentration of 4 %, beyond which ΔI decreased. Thus a 4 % concentration of HCHO was selected as optimum. The effect of the NAg-CDs concentration on the intensity of CL system was investigated (Fig. S4c). The largest ΔI was observed at a 1:30 dilution ratio of NAg-CDs/water (v/v). Therefore, 1:30 of NAg-CDs/water (v/v) was selected as the best concentration for subsequent studies. Finally, the influence of H2SO4 in the range of 0.05–0.35 M was studied. Fig. S4d showed that the ΔI gradually increased in the range of 0.05–0.2 M, and decreased when the concentration was greater than 0.2 M. Therefore, 0.2 M H2SO4 was selected as the optimal concentration. The effect of flow rate for reaction reagents was investigated (Fig. S4e). The CL emission intensity was found to increase with increasing pumping velocity, due to the rapid rate of the CL reaction. The obtained results showed that the flow rate of 2.8 mL/min in the main pump (P2) was the best flow rate for the CL system, which was selected for all further studies.

As trace amounts of TA considerably diminished the CL intensity of the NAg-CDs-KMnO4-HCHO system, a sensitive method for the determination of TA was consequently developed. Under the optimum conditions, the effect of TA concentration on the NAg-CDs-KMnO4-HCHO CL system was investigated. As shown in Fig. 3a, the CL intensity decreased with increasing concentrations of TA. Fig. 3b showed a good linear relationship between the ∆I and the logarithm of TA concentrations in the range of 0.4–10.0 μM. The regression equation was ∆I = 734.8 + 850.2 lg [TA] with an R2 of 0.9983, where [TA] represented the concentration of TA in μM. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was 1.4 for eleven determinations of 2.0 μM of TA. The limit of detection (LOD) was 43.0 nM at signal-to-noise of 3. Compared with other methods, our proposed method showed a lower or comparable detection limit compared with most of the previous methods for detecting TA, as shown in Table S1. Moreover, this method extended the application of CDs co-doped with metallic and nonmetallic elements in CL systems as well as food analysis.

In order to evaluate the practicability of the established method for TA detection, the effects of some potential coexisting substances in real samples were usually considered before the assay. Some potential interfering substances with concentration of 2.0 μΜ TA were added to the NAg-CDs-KMnO4-HCHO system at the same time to estimate the interfering stability of this method, and it was assumed that the tolerable limit of an interfering substance was a relative error of at most ±5 % in peak height at a concentration of 2.0 μM TA. The result is summarized in Table S2. Furthermore, the concentrations of most potential interfering species in the tea and wine samples were either within tolerable limits or could be mitigated through dilution, ensuring no interference in TA determination. These results demonstrated the robustness of our method and its feasibility for TA detection in tea and wine samples.

To further verify the feasibility of the method in real samples, the procedure for the determination of TA in tea and wine samples was applied with standard addition and recovery method to eliminate the matrix effect. The step of sample preparation was described in Section 2. Table S3 showed the detection of 3.11 and 1.58 μM TA in the prepared tea and wine samples, respectively. After the addition of 1.0 and 2.0 μM TA, a recovery of 98.6–104.9 % was obtained, indicating that the efficient of the method for the determination of TA in tea and wine.

The CL spectra of the NAg-CDs-KMnO4-HCHO, NAg-CDs-KMnO4, and KMnO4-HCHO systems were studied using some high-energy cutoff filters in combination with an FI system. As shown in Fig. 2b, two emission peaks were observed in the spectra of the NAg-CDs-KMnO4 system and the NAg-CDs-KMnO4-HCHO system. One peak at 500 nm corresponded to the FL peak of NAg-CDs at 430 nm excitation. The other was after 620 nm, which was consistent with the CL of Mn(II)* (Adcock et al., 2008; Barnett et al., 2002). Therefore, it was concluded that the same emitter species, including NAg-CDs* and Mn(II)*, was responsible for the emission in both cases. In the KMnO4-HCHO system, only a weak amplitude occurred after 620 nm, indicating the formation of a small amount of Mn(II)* in the system. The reaction between acidic KMnO4 and HCHO can generate the intermediate product permanganate ester (Jaky and Szammer, 1997). When NAg-CDs were added into the KMnO4-HCHO system, they can facilitate the oxidative cleavage of permanganate ester, accelerating the generation of Mn(III), consequently resulting in higher CL intensity (Khataee et al., 2016; Manzoori et al., 2011). This suggests that NAg-CDs possess excellent catalytic property for the KMnO4-HCHO reaction. The role of HCHO is likely to facilitate the reduction of acidic KMnO4 to small amount of Mn(II)*, triggering CL, similar to background CL in KMnO4-HCHO reactions.

To gain better insight into the principle of the CL behavior and to monitor the intermediates, the effects of various common radical scavengers were used to determine the roles of hydroxyl radicals (•OH), superoxide anion radicals (•O2 −), and singlet oxygen (1O2) on CL intensity. In this study, four scavengers were tested, including NaN3, nitrotetrazolium blue chloride (NBT), thiourea, and ascorbic acid (AA). As shown in Fig. 2c, the CL signal was almost completely inhibited by the addition AA to the proposed CL system, indicating that the presence of reactive oxygen species played a key role in generating the emitting species (Lin et al., 2011; Zhang et al., 2012). Meanwhile, NaN3 had no effect on the CL signals, suggesting that the observed CL was not caused by 1O2 (Shi et al., 2013). Interestingly, the CL signals were strongly attenuated upon the addition of NBT and thiourea (a typical scavenger for •O2 − and •OH, respectively) (Lin et al., 2011), indicating that •O2 − and •OH contributed to the CL emission of this system. Therefore, the emission of the NAg-CDs-KMnO4-HCHO system could originate from the electron-transfer annihilation of NAg-CDs•– and NAg-CDs•+ in the presence of oxidizing (•OH) and reducing (•O2 −) radicals.

Considering all the above experimental data, we proposed the reaction mechanism of the NAg-CDs-KMnO4-HCHO CL system as shown in Fig. 4. Reaction of radicals with NAg-CDs played an important role. •OH injected holes into the NAg-CDs and generated NAg-CDs•+, while •O2 – served as an electron donors, providing electrons into the NAg-CDs and formed NAg-CDs•–. Electron transfer annihilation of NAg-CDs•+ and NAg-CDs•– resulted in the formation of excited-state NAg-CDs*, which acted as the final emitter in the system. When NAg-CDs* returned to the ground state upon photon irradiation, CL emission occurred (Shah et al., 2016; Xue et al., 2011).

The CL spectra in Fig. 2b reveal that the presence of TA predominantly influences the CL intensity of the NAg-CDs-KMnO4-HCHO system while maintaining the spectral trend and shape. The radical scavenging experiments demonstrated that the reduction in CL intensity upon the addition of TA is primarily attributable to TA’s capacity to scavenge reactive oxygen species (Fig. 2c).

In this study, NAg-CDs were demonstrated to significantly enhance the CL intensity of the KMnO4-HCHO system, while TA was identified as an inhibitor of CL within the developed sensing platform. Therefore, a simple, rapid, and sensitive FI-CL method was developed for the detection of TA in tea and wine samples. This study conclusively highlights NAg-CDs as highly efficient CL catalysts and emitters, emphasizing their significant potential in CL analysis.
Written informed consent for publication of this paper was obtained from the Shanxi Normal University and all authors.

Zixuan Wang: Investigation, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Jiaoyan Xie: Conceptualization, Supervision, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Suqin Han: Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jfca.2023.105792.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Adcock et al., 2014

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Advances in the use of acidic potassium permanganate as a chemiluminescence reagent: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Adcock, J.L., Barnett, N.W., Barrow, C.J., Francis, P.S., 2014. Advances in the use of acidic potassium permanganate as a chemiluminescence reagent: A review. Anal. Chim. Acta 807, 9–28. http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2013.11.016.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Adcock et al., 2008

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The characteristic red chemiluminescence from reactions with acidic potassium permanganate: further spectroscopic evidence for a manganese(II) emitter

The characteristic red chemiluminescence from reactions with acidic potassium permanganate: further spectroscopic evidence for a manganese(II) emitter. Analyst 133, 49–51. https://doi.org/10. 1039/b714147e.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Amjadi et al., 2018

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An amplified chemiluminescence system based on Si-doped carbon dots for detection of catecholamines

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Amjadi, M., Hallaj, T., Manzoori, J.L., Shahbazsaghir, T., 2018. An amplified chemiluminescence system based on Si-doped carbon dots for detection of catecholamines. Spectrochim. Acta A 201, 223–228. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2018.04.058.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Ansari et al., 2021

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Doped-carbon dots: recent advances in their biosensing, bioimaging and therapy applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ansari, L., Hallaj, S., Hallaj, T., Amjadi, M., 2021. Doped-carbon dots: recent advances in their biosensing, bioimaging and therapy applications. Colloid. Surface B 203, 111743. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2021.111743.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Baker and Baker, 2010
ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Baker, S.N., Baker, G.A., 2010. Luminescent carbon nanodots: emergent nanolights. Angew. Chem., Int. Ed. 49, 6726. https://doi.org/10.1002/anie.200906623.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Barnett et al., 2002
